statement: For a basis, injectivity of `f` is equivalent to linear independence of `f` on the basis vectors.
lean_statement:
theorem injective_iff_linearIndependent_of_isBasis
    {K V : Type*} [Field K] [AddCommGroup V] [Module K V]
    {n : Nat} (v : Fin n → V)
    (hv : LinearIndependent K v ∧ Submodule.span K (Set.range v) = ⊤)
    (f : V →ₗ[K] V) :
    Function.Injective f ↔ LinearIndependent K (fun i => f (v i))
proof:
Proof: assume `f` is injective. Then a linear relation `∑ c_i • f(v i) = 0` can be rewritten as
`f(∑ c_i • v i) = 0`. Injectivity forces `∑ c_i • v i = 0`, and since the `v i` are linearly
independent, all `c_i = 0`. Hence `f(v i)` are linearly independent.

Conversely, assume `f(v i)` are linearly independent. If `f x = 0`, write `x` as a linear
combination of the basis vectors `v i` (since they span `V`). Applying `f` gives a relation among
the `f(v i)`, so all coefficients are `0`, hence `x = 0`. Thus `ker f = {0}` and `f` is injective.
